questions.question_text: In Alabama single agency, which set of duties does the licensee owe to the client?
questions.correct_answer: Full fiduciary duties including loyalty and confidentiality
distractors: Only honesty and good faith obligations
Limited duties of disclosure and accounting only
Duties determined by the qualifying broker alone
questions.explanation: Single agency in Alabama means the licensee owes full fiduciary duties to the client: loyalty, confidentiality, obedience, disclosure, accounting, and reasonable care. This is the highest level of duty a licensee can owe and distinguishes client relationships from customer relationships.

questions.question_text: A listing broker's client has a property listed for sale. Another brokerage's licensee agrees to act on behalf of the listing broker's client. What role does this licensee serve?
distractors: Dual agent for both parties
Transaction broker for the buyer
Designated agent for the seller
questions.explanation: Subagency occurs when a licensee from another brokerage acts on behalf of the listing broker's client (the seller). The subagent owes fiduciary duties to the listing broker's client, not to the buyer. This is distinct from dual agency, transaction brokerage, or designated agency.

questions.question_text: A single agent learns that the client's property has a structural defect. Which fiduciary duty requires the agent to inform the client?
distractors: The duty of obedience
The duty of accounting
The duty of loyalty
questions.explanation: The duty of disclosure requires the agent to reveal all material facts and information that could affect the client's decisions. A structural defect is a material fact the client needs to know. While loyalty and other duties also apply in single agency, disclosure is the specific duty that mandates sharing this information.

questions.question_text: Which fiduciary duty requires a single agent to properly handle client funds and maintain accurate records?
distractors: The duty of loyalty
The duty of reasonable care
The duty of obedience
questions.explanation: The duty of accounting requires the agent to properly handle all funds entrusted to them and maintain accurate records of financial transactions related to the client's property. This includes earnest money deposits and any other funds received on behalf of the client.

questions.question_text: A subagent discovers that the buyer is willing to pay more than the listed price. To whom does the subagent owe the duty to disclose this information?
questions.correct_answer: The listing broker's client, the seller
distractors: The buyer who shared the information
Both the buyer and the seller equally
Only the subagent's own qualifying broker
questions.explanation: A subagent acts on behalf of the listing broker's client (the seller) and owes fiduciary duties to that client. Information about the buyer's willingness to pay more is material to the seller and must be disclosed. The subagent does not owe fiduciary duties to the buyer, only honesty and fair dealing.

questions.question_text: What is required before a licensee may act as a dual agent in Alabama?
distractors: Verbal agreement from both parties
Commission approval on a case-by-case basis
Only the qualifying broker's written permission
questions.explanation: Limited consensual dual agency in Alabama requires written consent from ALL parties to the transaction. Verbal agreements are insufficient. The consent must be documented in writing before the licensee can represent both the buyer and the seller in the same transaction.

questions.question_text: Under 2025 changes, when does dual agency apply in Alabama?
questions.correct_answer: When one agent represents both buyer and seller
distractors: When two agents in the same firm represent each side
Whenever buyer and seller use the same brokerage
Only in commercial transactions over $500,000
questions.explanation: Under the 2025 changes, dual agency in Alabama is now limited to situations where ONE agent represents both the buyer and the seller. When two different licensees within the same brokerage each represent one party, that is designated single agency, not dual agency. This is a significant clarification from prior practice.

questions.question_text: Two licensees work under the same qualifying broker. One represents the seller and the other represents the buyer in the same transaction. What type of agency relationship exists?
distractors: Dual agency requiring consent from both parties
Transaction brokerage for both parties
Subagency on behalf of the seller
questions.explanation: When two licensees under the same qualifying broker each represent one side of a transaction, this is designated single agency. Each licensee owes full fiduciary duties to their own client. Under the 2025 changes, this is NOT dual agency because two different agents are involved, each exclusively representing one party.

questions.question_text: A dual agent in Alabama receives a confidential offer strategy from the buyer. Can the agent share this with the seller?
questions.correct_answer: No, a dual agent cannot advocate for one over the other
distractors: Yes, the duty of disclosure requires sharing all information
Only if the seller specifically requests it in writing
Yes, both parties consented to full information sharing
questions.explanation: A dual agent in Alabama operates under limited consensual dual agency, meaning the agent cannot advocate for one party over the other. Sharing the buyer's confidential offer strategy would give the seller an unfair advantage. The agent must maintain neutrality between both parties despite representing both.

questions.question_text: In designated single agency, what level of duty does each designated licensee owe to their assigned client?
questions.correct_answer: Full fiduciary duties to their own client
distractors: Limited duties similar to transaction brokerage
Shared fiduciary duties with the other designated agent
Only honesty and good faith to both parties
questions.explanation: In designated single agency, each designated licensee owes full fiduciary duties (loyalty, confidentiality, obedience, disclosure, accounting, reasonable care) exclusively to their own assigned client. This preserves the fiduciary relationship even when both agents work under the same qualifying broker.

questions.question_text: In transaction brokerage, what is the relationship between the licensee and the parties?
questions.correct_answer: The parties are customers, not clients
distractors: Both parties are clients with full fiduciary rights
The buyer is a client and the seller is a customer
The relationship depends on who pays the commission
questions.explanation: In transaction brokerage, the licensee assists both parties as customers, not clients. There is no fiduciary relationship with either party. The transaction broker must treat all parties honestly, in good faith, and without favoritism, but does not owe the elevated fiduciary duties that come with a client relationship.

questions.question_text: A transaction broker learns that a buyer is desperate to close quickly and would accept unfavorable terms. What obligation does the broker have regarding this information?
questions.correct_answer: The broker must not share it or favor either party
distractors: Disclose it to the seller as a material fact
Share it with both parties for full transparency
Report it to the qualifying broker for guidance
questions.explanation: A transaction broker must treat all parties honestly, in good faith, and without favoritism. Sharing one party's vulnerabilities with the other would constitute favoritism. Since there is no fiduciary relationship, there is no duty to disclose a party's negotiating weaknesses to the other side.

questions.question_text: What is the key distinction between a client and a customer in Alabama real estate?
questions.correct_answer: Clients receive fiduciary duties; customers get honesty only
distractors: Clients pay commission; customers do not
Clients sign contracts; customers use verbal agreements
Clients are buyers; customers are sellers
questions.explanation: The fundamental distinction in Alabama real estate is the level of duty owed. Clients are owed full fiduciary duties (loyalty, confidentiality, obedience, disclosure, accounting, reasonable care) through a single agency or designated agency relationship. Customers are owed only honesty and good faith, as in transaction brokerage.

questions.question_text: A seller asks a transaction broker to recommend a listing price that will outperform competing properties. How should the broker respond?
questions.correct_answer: Decline, as advocating for one party is not permitted
distractors: Provide the advice as part of standard brokerage services
Offer the advice only with the buyer's written consent
Provide the advice but also help the buyer negotiate down
questions.explanation: A transaction broker cannot advocate for one party over the other. Providing strategic pricing advice designed to give the seller a competitive advantage goes beyond the transaction broker's role of treating all parties without favoritism. This type of advocacy is reserved for agents in a fiduciary client relationship.

questions.question_text: A transaction broker discovers a known roof leak during a property showing. Must the broker disclose this to the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, honesty requires disclosing known defects
distractors: No, the broker has no fiduciary duty to the buyer
Only if the seller authorizes the disclosure
Only if the buyer asks about the roof condition
questions.explanation: Although a transaction broker does not owe fiduciary duties, the obligation to treat parties honestly and in good faith requires disclosure of known material defects like a roof leak. Honesty and good faith are baseline duties in all Alabama real estate relationships, regardless of the agency type.
